title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post · Amnesty International · AEI · Moscow Times · Kyiv Independent

regions.name: Middle East
overview: The US-Iran ceasefire has fully collapsed into open war — the US is in its sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iranian territory (Bandar Abbas, Bushehr, Chabahar, Jask, Qeshm, Abu Musa, and now targets in northern Iran), has reinstated a full naval blockade of Iranian ports, and Iran is retaliating by striking commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and firing missiles toward Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar.

Yemen's four-year Saudi-Houthi truce has broken down for the first time after the 13 July Sanaa-Abha exchange, while separate Houthi-government fighting inside Yemen saw its worst clashes in four years (Hodeidah, 5 Jul); Houthi leaders are now threatening a 'siege' of Saudi Arabia and closure of Bab al-Mandeb.

Gaza's and Lebanon's ceasefires remain largely nominal — Gaza's cumulative death toll since the October truce has passed 1,100 amid near-daily strikes, while Lebanon saw lethal Israeli drone strikes through 10 July before a partial 'pilot zone' withdrawal was announced on 15 July.

Iran-aligned proxy activity and settler violence are intensifying on secondary fronts — Iraqi militias struck Erbil airport and the US consulate (15 Jul) after 600+ attacks on US forces since spring, while West Bank settler violence is running at what the UN calls a record pace.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 9 Jul — Gaza-wide: Israeli strikes killed roughly 10 Palestinians in a single 24-hour period despite the nominal October 2025 ceasefire.
15 Jul — Jabaliya refugee camp: An Israeli airstrike on a police station killed a woman and six police officers.
16 Jul — Khan Younis/Muwasi/Rafah: Strikes and tank fire killed at least five Palestinians, including three members of one family and a child near Rafah.
countries.coa: The October 2025 ceasefire remains nominally in force but is hollow — Gaza's Government Media Office now tallies 3,689 recorded ceasefire violations and 1,122 Palestinians killed since the truce took effect, against 5 Israeli soldiers killed in the same period. CRITICAL maintained; watch whether the daily strike tempo falls or a third-party monitoring mechanism gains traction.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 6 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa: An Israeli drone strike on a vehicle killed 4, including a school principal, her mother, a domestic worker and a Syrian national.
8 Jul — Bint Jbeil district: Israeli airstrikes hit multiple sites (Ali al-Taher, Beit Yahoun, Kunin, Baraachit) ahead of new Rome-track talks.
10 Jul — Kafr Rumman: An Israeli drone strike killed a man riding a motorcycle.
15 Jul — Southern Lebanon: The US State Department announced Israel agreed to withdraw from remaining 'pilot zone' outposts — a partial de-escalation signal.
countries.coa: Intermittent Israeli drone and airstrikes on south Lebanon continued through mid-July despite the June 26 framework deal, though the 15 July announcement of a planned Israeli withdrawal from pilot-zone outposts is a genuine, if partial, de-escalatory signal. CRITICAL maintained given continued lethal strikes and the risk Hezbollah is drawn into the wider Iran war.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 8-9 Jul — Bandar Abbas, Sirik, Qeshm, Hengam Island, Jask: US strikes hit IRGC and army sites after the ceasefire collapsed.

13-14 Jul — Bushehr, Chabahar, Jask, Konarak, Abu Musa, Abadan, Mahshahr: A five-hour US strike campaign hit military and oil infrastructure; the US reinstated a full naval blockade of Iranian ports effective 14 July.
16 Jul — Northern Iran/Gulf-wide: US strikes expanded into northern Iran on a sixth consecutive night; Iran disabled a tanker attempting to run the blockade.
Ongoing — Strait of Hormuz: CENTCOM says Iran has struck 7 commercial vessels, causing close to a dozen civilian crew casualties, while firing missiles toward Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, trending worse — this is a live, escalating war rather than a paused conflict: six consecutive nights of US strikes, a full naval blockade, and combat now spreading to northern Iran and neighboring states. Absent a breakthrough in stalled Qatari/Pakistani-mediated talks (deadlocked over Hormuz sovereignty), expect continued nightly strikes, further Iranian shipping and proxy attacks, and elevated risk the war widens further via Iraqi militias, the Houthis or renewed missile fire on Israel/Gulf states.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 5 Jul — Hodeidah: A Houthi mortar, drone and sniper attack on government forces killed 16-22 troops — the worst internal fighting in four years.
13 Jul — Sanaa International Airport: The runway was struck (attribution disputed) to block an Iranian plane returning a Houthi delegation from Khamenei's funeral.
13-14 Jul — Abha International Airport, Saudi Arabia: Houthis retaliated with a ballistic missile and drone strike, ending the four-year Saudi-Houthi truce.
14 Jul — Regional: Houthi leadership threatened a 'siege' and a 'different war' against Saudi Arabia, naming oil infrastructure as a target and floating a Bab al-Mandeb closure.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The 2022 Saudi-Houthi truce has effectively collapsed and Yemen's internal Houthi-government front separately saw its worst clashes in four years. Expect renewed two-track escalation — internal fighting around Hodeidah/Marib and external Houthi-Saudi exchanges — with real risk of Bab al-Mandeb disruption and renewed Red Sea shipping attacks.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: Through 16 Jul — Nationwide: Israel remains a direct combatant in the widening Iran war, exposed to continued Iranian missile fire; cumulative toll includes roughly 650 Iranian missiles fired and 24 killed in Israel/West Bank since the war began.
11 Jul — West Bank: Israeli settlers attacked foreign journalists covering an anniversary event.
16 Jul — West Bank-wide: At least 20 settler-violence incidents were reported over a single weekend.
Ongoing — near Hebron: A 16-year-old Palestinian boy was shot in the chest by Israeli forces in Bani Naim (stable condition).
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Israel remains engaged on three simultaneous fronts — direct exposure to Iran's war and renewed missile salvos, near-daily Gaza strikes despite the nominal ceasefire, and record-pace West Bank settler violence that the UN now calls the highest rate on record. No near-term resolution is visible on any of the three tracks.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 15 Jul — Erbil: Six drones targeted Erbil International Airport and the US consulate; all intercepted, attribution unclear between Iran and Iraqi militias.
14 Jul — Baghdad: US Defense Secretary Hegseth told PM al-Zaidi that Iran-aligned militias have conducted 600+ attacks on US forces since spring.
Ongoing — Iraq-wide: Kataib Hezbollah, Harakat Hezbollah al-Nujaba and Asaib Ahl al-Haq continue targeting US-linked sites including Al Asad Air Base.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Iraq continues edging toward becoming an active frontline of the US-Iran war via militia drone and rocket attacks. Expect continued intermittent attacks tracking the wider war's intensity, US pressure on Baghdad to disarm militias, and risk that a major incident triggers direct US retaliation on Iraqi soil.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 13 Jul — Abha International Airport: A Houthi missile and drone salvo was intercepted; no casualties reported.
14 Jul — Nationwide: Houthi officials threatened a 'siege' and a 'different war' against Saudi Arabia, naming oil facilities as targets and floating closure of Bab al-Mandeb.
Ongoing — Yemen border: This marks the first Saudi-Houthi combat exchange since the 2022 truce, following the blocked Sanaa delegation flight.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward CRITICAL. The four-year Saudi-Houthi truce has broken down; expect continued threats and likely further limited Houthi missile/drone launches against Saudi territory, a hardened Saudi military posture on the Yemen border, and elevated Red Sea/Bab al-Mandeb shipping risk given the direct threats to oil infrastructure.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 1 Jul — Damascus: A bombing near a lawyers' cafe by the judicial complex killed 10 and wounded 21.
7 Jul — Four Seasons Hotel area, Damascus: Twin IEDs detonated during French President Macron's state visit, killing 1 and wounding 36; Macron was unharmed.
9-10 Jul — Damascus and countryside: Authorities announced the arrest of the ISIL-linked cell behind both bombings following coordinated raids across four neighborhoods.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Despite this cell's arrest, ISIL retains an estimated 1,500-3,000 fighters inside Syria capable of further opportunistic attacks, and continued unauthorized Israeli settlement expansion into Syrian territory adds a second axis of instability. Watch whether further cells are uncovered and whether the post-Assad transitional government's legitimacy holds.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: Ongoing — Sinai: No dated kinetic security incidents were found this week; the peninsula remains free of major attacks.
Ongoing — Suez Canal: Traffic remains roughly 60% below pre-crisis levels; new transit surcharges took effect mid-July as carriers continue favoring the Cape of Good Hope route over Red Sea risk.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No direct kinetic incident on Egyptian soil this week; Egypt's main exposure is economic rather than security-driven, and its risk profile will likely continue tracking the Yemen/Red Sea situation — a Bab al-Mandeb closure threat would hit canal revenue hard.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 9 Jul — Jordanian airspace: 8 of 10 Iranian ballistic missiles were intercepted; Iran claimed it targeted a US command center and Al-Azraq air base.
15 Jul — Jordanian airspace: 3 additional Iranian ballistic missiles were intercepted; no casualties or damage.
16 Jul — Jordanian airspace: 8 more Iranian missiles were intercepted; Jordan has now intercepted 261 of 281 Iranian projectiles since late February.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Jordan remains a recurring secondary target for Iranian missile fire aimed at US-linked infrastructure. Expect continued intermittent launches tracking the wider war's intensity, sustained strain on interceptor stocks, and low but nonzero risk of a leaker causing casualties given the sustained volume of fire.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 8 Jul — Al-Furnaj/Souq Al-Jumaa, Tripoli: Armed clashes broke out between rival militias over territorial control.
Ongoing — Tripoli: Militia buildup continues, with GNU-aligned forces holding western/southern districts and the rival Special Deterrence Force (al-Radaa) holding eastern districts.
Ongoing — Sirte: East-west military unification talks continue in parallel with the militia standoff.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Expect continued sporadic clashes and force posturing in Tripoli; absent a triggering event such as an assassination, full-scale conflict is not yet imminent, but risk remains elevated alongside the parallel, still-unresolved unification track.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 4 Jul — Tunis: Ennahda secretary-general Ajmi Ourimi and member Mosaab Gharbi were each sentenced to 3 years on terrorism-related charges.

8 Jul — Tunis: 21 people, including Ennahda leaders and former officials, were sentenced to 12-35 years on terrorism/state-security charges; exiled former president Rached Ghannouchi received 14 years in absentia.
8 Jul — Geneva/Tunis: Human Rights Watch published a report on the 'intensifying' crackdown; activist Sihem Bensedrine was separately sentenced to 25 years.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No terrorism/kinetic incident this week, but the intensifying political crackdown — mass sentencing, further prosecutions, international criticism — remains a notable political-stability risk factor worth close monitoring.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Ukraine's deep-strike refinery campaign has now hit all 11 of Russia's largest gasoline producers (Bashkortostan's Gazprom Neftekhim Salavat and Krasnodar's Afipsky refinery struck 13-14 July), pushing Russia's fuel crisis to affect 55+ of 83 regions with a daily shortfall near 25,000 tonnes.

Russia intensified retaliatory strikes on Ukrainian port infrastructure and, for the first time, began targeting merchant vessels directly at anchor or underway in the greater Odesa area (Chornomorsk, Yuzhny, Izmail), alongside continued deadly strikes on Sumy, Zaporizhzhia and Kyiv.

Kyiv underwent its most significant wartime cabinet shake-up in months — Zelensky dismissed Defense Minister Fedorov (15 Jul) and Naftogaz chief Koretskyi was confirmed as new PM (16 Jul), triggering street protests and a military resignation-in-protest even as the war's tempo stayed at its peak.

Political transitions continued across the wider region — Moldova's PM nominee Vasile Tofan faces a 15-day confirmation clock amid a fresh Russian drone incursion, Georgia's opposition leader was sentenced to 13 years as protests passed 594 consecutive days, and Armenia-Azerbaijan border delimitation advanced with a newly supportive signal from Russia.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 13-14 Jul — Bashkortostan/Krasnodar: Ukraine struck the Gazprom Neftekhim Salavat refinery and the Afipsky refinery — the last of Russia's 11 largest gasoline producers, per Ukraine's General Staff.

10-16 Jul — Greater Odesa (Chornomorsk, Yuzhny, Izmail): A sustained Russian missile/drone campaign against port infrastructure and, for the first time, merchant vessels directly struck roughly 15 ships in under a week; a 16 Jul missile strike on Odesa killed 2 and wounded 8, a 15 Jul strike on a residential building killed 3.
13 Jul — Sumy Oblast: 13 people were injured in fresh Russian strikes, continuing a pattern that killed 5 (including a child) on 3 July.

15-16 Jul — Kyiv: Zelensky dismissed Defense Minister Fedorov, architect of the drone-war program, triggering nationwide protests; parliament confirmed Naftogaz CEO Serhiy Koretskyi as new PM the following day.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, deepening. The refinery campaign has now struck all 11 of Russia's top gasoline producers while Russia has answered with an intensified port and shadow-fleet-adjacent strike campaign against Odesa. The Fedorov dismissal and PM reshuffle introduce a period of domestic political friction the Kremlin may look to probe; Kostiantynivka remains a grinding, low-tempo front with Lyman the likely next Russian objective.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 9-10 Jul — Moscow: An overnight Ukrainian drone attack forced Domodedovo and other Moscow airports to suspend flights, part of a reported 1,200+ drones approaching Moscow over a seven-day span.
13 Jul — Moscow region/border areas: Ukrainian drone strikes killed at least 4 across Russia; Moscow's airports again imposed flight restrictions.

9-15 Jul — Nationwide: Fuel shortages/rationing now affect 55+ of 83 regions, with a daily shortfall near 25,000 tonnes; at least 6 regions run odd-even purchase schemes and Siberian farmers warn the autumn harvest is at risk from diesel shortages.
16 Jul — Donbas front: Russian forces pressed toward Lyman following the Kostiantynivka capture, while resuming Black Sea port strikes partly in response to Ukraine's shadow-fleet operations.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained; the fuel crisis has become genuinely nationwide with real civil-unrest potential given rationing and price shocks. Expect the refining-capacity crisis to deepen further, with rationing spreading and possible agricultural knock-on effects around the autumn harvest, alongside continued large-scale Ukrainian drone waves against Moscow-area airports.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 26 Jun-2 Jul — Nationwide: Belarus shut down the four Russian-installed drone relay/communication towers on its territory following Kyiv's ultimatum, though it remains unclear whether the equipment was fully dismantled.
15 Jul — Malyn, Ukraine: A Russian Shahed drone struck a gas station, raising renewed concern Moscow may be routing long-range drone guidance back through Belarusian territory despite the shutdown.
6 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko again denied any intent to send troops into Ukraine while accusing an unnamed international 'war party' of prolonging the conflict.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Belarus continues to walk a line of technical compliance with the relay-station shutdown while avoiding direct entanglement; watch for recurring accusations that Russia is reactivating or rerouting relay infrastructure through Belarusian territory, which could reignite the ultimatum dynamic.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 11 Jul — Chișinău: President Sandu nominated businessman Vasile Tofan as PM; a 15-day constitutional confirmation clock is now running (through roughly 26 July).
12-13 Jul — Copanca, Căușeni district: A Russian Shahed-type drone crossed into Moldovan airspace and crashed near the village during a Russian strike on Odesa Oblast; no casualties.
14 Jul — Chișinău: Moldova's Foreign Ministry summoned Russia's ambassador and issued a formal protest note over the sovereignty violation.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Tofan's confirmation is likely to proceed within the 15-day window given PAS's parliamentary control, though continued sporadic Russian drone incursions tied to the nearby Odesa strike campaign reinforce Moldova's direct spillover risk.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 10 Jul — Tbilisi: Opposition 'Strong Georgia' leader Aleko Elisashvili was sentenced to 13 years in prison for 'attempted terrorism,' a ruling denounced as politically motivated even by Georgia's own Public Defender.
10-14 Jul — Tbilisi: Pro-EU protests passed their 590th and then 594th consecutive day, demanding release of political prisoners and free elections.

Ongoing — Tbilisi/Brussels: Amnesty International's 'Anatomy of Repression' report documented a coordinated state system of arbitrary detention and 150+ politically motivated imprisonments since the protest movement began.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The Georgian Dream government is likely to continue and intensify judicial suppression of opposition figures following the Elisashvili precedent as protests pass the 600-day mark, with sustained Western condemnation unlikely to produce material policy change from Tbilisi.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 9 Jul — Armenia-Azerbaijan border: Roughly 13km of the border is now delimited and demarcated, with commissions continuing north-to-south work.

9-10 Jul — Yerevan: PM Pashinyan announced a 20-day break while remaining engaged on security issues, following a 3 July working visit to Tehran and continued participation in Russia's Innoprom 2026 exhibition.

16 Jul — Moscow/Yerevan: Russia's Foreign Ministry stated it is 'ready to support' the Armenia-Azerbaijan peace treaty and unblocking of regional transport links — a notable shift given Moscow's traditionally ambivalent role.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No kinetic incidents this window; incremental border delimitation and diplomatic momentum continue, though formal treaty signature remains gated on Armenia's constitutional amendment process, expected to take months rather than weeks.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 13 Jul — Shusha: President Aliyev said Azerbaijan is 'seriously considering' complete withdrawal from the Council of Europe, prompting personal outreach from the Council's Secretary General.
13 Jul — Shusha: Aliyev reaffirmed peace with Armenia is 'achievable' while citing expanded gas exports, now supplying 16 countries including new deliveries to Germany and Austria.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. The Council of Europe withdrawal threat functions as diplomatic leverage rather than an imminent formal exit; Azerbaijan continues deepening its role as an alternative gas supplier to the EU alongside its peace-process rhetoric.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: Siege warfare has become the Sahel's defining tactic — El Obeid (Sudan) and multiple Burkinabè towns are both experiencing blockade conditions where civilians are trapped, with UN bodies in both cases invoking the risk of a repeat of the El Fasher massacre.

France's institutional exit from the Sahel is essentially complete — Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations on 26 June, completing a trend already seen in Mali and Niger, while Russia's Africa Corps has become the load-bearing military partner, its convoys, drones and airpower decisive in relieving Anéfis.

Strain inside security forces themselves is surfacing, even where unconfirmed — Niger's disputed 'small mutiny' report over Tillabéri deployment conditions sits alongside juntas across the region projecting confidence in territorial-consolidation narratives that lack independent corroboration.

Cross-border spillover is accelerating humanitarian collapse — RSF attacks on Zaghawa villages near Tine are driving fresh refugee flows into a Chadian response already short $428 million, while jihadist groups and criminal banditry increasingly operate across a shared Sahel-Lake Chad Basin corridor.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 4-9 Jul — Anéfis/Gao region: The JNIM-Azawad Liberation Front coalition attacked Anéfis, Aguelhok, Gao, Sévaré and a prison south of Bamako; Anéfis was briefly seized before FAMa/Africa Corps relief convoys broke the encirclement, losing a helicopter en route before retaking the town.
10 Jul — Bamako: Mali's armed forces chief of staff confirmed roughly 30 FAMa soldiers killed and 60 wounded during the Anéfis operation.
Ongoing through 17 Jul — Kidal, Tessalit, Tessit and Ber remain outside government control, held by the JNIM-FLA coalition, which has stated its aim of also taking Gao, Ménaka and Timbuktu.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Expect a period of relative consolidation as both sides absorb losses from the 4-9 July fighting, with FAMa/Africa Corps prioritizing the Gao-Anéfis corridor while the coalition consolidates around Kidal. A renewed coordinated offensive on the scale of early July is plausible within 90 days, especially timed to Malian army vulnerabilities.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 3-4 Jul — Niamey: The Presidential Guard, backed by armored vehicles, reinforced the State House after unconfirmed reports of an armored unit refusing frontline deployment to Tillabéri; the government has issued no official confirmation or denial.

Ongoing — Tillabéri: The region remains the deadliest civilian conflict zone in the central Sahel following a string of mass-casualty JNIM/ISSP attacks in May and June (Garbougna, Inatès, Banibangou, the Niamey airport assault).
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The unresolved and unconfirmed loyalty crisis inside the security apparatus, layered onto sustained JNIM/ISSP pressure across Tillabéri, represents a compounding risk to regime stability beyond territorial control; treat continued government silence as a negative indicator.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 26 Jun — Ouagadougou: The junta severed diplomatic relations with France, accusing Paris of 'neo-colonial ambitions and active support for subversive networks and terrorists.'

30 Jun — Geneva: The UN Human Rights Office announced permanent closure of its Burkina Faso operations after the government suspended its local mandate, ending independent international documentation of abuses.

6 Jul — Nationwide: A New Humanitarian investigation found Burkinabè soldiers are actively preventing civilians from leaving jihadist-besieged towns, blocking evacuation convoys and threatening those who try to leave.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The humanitarian access crisis is likely to deepen rather than resolve now that UN human rights monitoring has closed, raising the risk of unreported mass-casualty or starvation events in besieged towns like Djibo; the France rupture removes a channel that previously provided some transparency and leverage.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Nairobi/Geneva: Amnesty International and the UN Fact-Finding Mission concluded the RSF committed crimes against humanity, ethnic cleansing and acts bearing 'the hallmarks of genocide' during the fall of El Fasher.
5 Jul — Ambara-Tine road, North Darfur: RSF attacked six villages targeting Zaghawa communities, burning homes and markets and forcing thousands to flee toward Chad.

6-10 Jul — El Obeid: Near-daily RSF drone strikes hit critical infrastructure, including the main power substation, causing a city-wide blackout; the UN Security Council urged RSF to halt the assault, warning of 'imminent risk of mass atrocities.'
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. El Obeid is the highest-probability site for a second El Fasher-scale atrocity — RSF appears to be replicating its prior playbook of encirclement, infrastructure strikes and attrition ahead of a ground assault, and now controls all approach routes except the eastern axis. Absent a negotiated humanitarian corridor, expect a ground assault attempt within 60-90 days and continued mass displacement toward Chad.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: 5 Jul — Eastern border: Fresh refugee arrivals from RSF's Tine-area village attacks in North Darfur added to Chad's existing caseload.

Ongoing — Nationwide: UNHCR and WFP jointly warned of a $428 million funding shortfall threatening 1.3 million Sudanese refugees, with food assistance already cut in half for most and 80,000 families without shelter.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Chad's refugee response is on a trajectory toward acute service collapse absent a major funding infusion, with continued RSF operations against Darfur border communities making further refugee inflows highly likely; risk of cross-border spillover remains elevated given the pattern of attacks abutting the border.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: 1 Jul — Nouakchott: Security forces violently dispersed a solidarity rally supporting prosecuted opposition lawmakers; anti-slavery activist and MP Biram Dah Abeid and his wife were injured, reportedly by chemical agents.
10 Jul — Nouakchott: The two prosecuted lawmakers, Mariem Cheikh Samba Dieng and Ghamou Salem Achour, were released.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The 10 July release is a modest de-escalatory signal, but the 1 July violent dispersal — including alleged chemical-agent use against a sitting MP — indicates the government's tolerance for opposition and anti-slavery activism remains low; expect continued selective prosecutions and periodic crackdowns on assembly.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: Ongoing (as of 13 Jul) — Borno State: At least 36 students and 1 staff member remain in captivity from the Lassa school abduction despite several partial rescues; combined with a separate May abduction, roughly 78 children/staff remain missing.
~10 Jul — Maiduguri: Troops repelled coordinated ISWAP infiltration attempts at Muna Garage, Shuwari Village and the Ajilari Cross axis.
9-14 Jul — Zamfara State: Joint security forces rescued an abducted INEC staff member and, on 14 July, 8 more kidnap victims near Kaura Namoda, following a firefight with armed bandits.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Lassa abduction crisis remains unresolved with dozens still missing, while ISWAP sustained repeated infiltration attempts around Maiduguri and Zamfara continues to see near-weekly kidnap-for-ransom and cattle-rustling incidents — two converging high-intensity fronts showing no sign of abating.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: China is normalizing higher-order strategic signaling as routine rather than crisis — the first-ever open-Pacific SLBM test (6 Jul), the Joint Sea-2026 exercise with Russia, continuous unmarked coast guard patrols near Taiwan, and a Senkaku standoff all landed within days of each other.

Pakistan is fighting a genuine three-front internal crisis — Operation Shaban in Balochistan (91+ militants killed since 5 Jul), a Pakistan-administered Kashmir unrest cycle culminating in a march deferred to 21 July, and a latent Afghan border dispute, with two hard deadlines (21 Jul march, 27 Jul AJK election) converging within days.

State violence against civilians via airpower remains the dominant human-cost driver in Myanmar, where junta airstrikes killed civilians in Karen State, Sagaing and Magway on nearly every day of the 12-16 July stretch even as ASEAN publicly demanded an end to the violence.

Middle-power security cooperation is visibly deepening in response to China — Japan's destroyer transfer to the Philippines, a 14-nation-plus-EU joint statement backing the 2016 arbitral ruling, and US-ROK-Japan trilateral reaffirmation all landed in the same window as China's SLBM test.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 3-7 Jul — Nationwide: Kim Jong Un personally observed cruise-missile tests from North Korea's new 5,000-ton destroyer, ordering completion of weapons evaluation before a September commissioning.

Early Jul — Pentagon: US, South Korean and Japanese military chiefs met and reaffirmed the goal of North Korea's 'complete denuclearization,' alongside continued DMZ fortification that Seoul disputes as an armistice violation.
14 Jul — Pyongyang: State media condemned growing Seoul-NATO cooperation and the US-ROK-Japan trilateral partnership; Kim's public appearances in H1 2026 nearly doubled versus H1 2025.
countries.coa: Downgraded to HIGH from CRITICAL — no new escalatory kinetic action was found this week; North Korea continues incremental naval weapons validation and DMZ fortification without crossing into direct provocation. Expect continued denial of dialogue overtures and stepped-up cyber operations as a lower-cost complement to physical signaling; a missile test timed to US-ROK exercises remains the most likely flashpoint.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 12 Jul — Ter Ra Gaw Kyoe village, Luthaw Township: Junta aircraft dropped 40 bombs, injuring four civilians aged 8-41.
13 Jul — Four villages, Karen State: A junta Y-12 aircraft dropped 22 bombs, killing two women aged 65 and 57.
13-14 Jul — Sagaing/Magway: Airstrikes killed at least four civilians; helicopters bombed Kan Yin village, killing a pregnant woman, her unborn child and another woman.
16 Jul — Nationwide: A junta airstrike killed two more women the same day ASEAN publicly demanded an end to the violence.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The junta continues substituting airpower for ground control it cannot sustain, producing a steady drumbeat of civilian casualties in Sagaing, Magway and Karen State as resistance forces consolidate territorial gains; the Arakan Army continues transitioning from insurgency to governance in Rakhine. International pressure remains rhetorical without material effect on the bombing campaign.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 5-16 Jul — Balochistan: Operation Shaban killed 91 militants (129 including other operations) in response to coordinated TTP/BLA attacks (4-8 Jul) that killed 42, mostly security personnel.
16 Jul — Mastung district: A coordinated militant attack on a Frontier Corps convoy killed 3 security personnel and injured 29 on the N-25 highway.

15 Jul — Pakistan-administered Kashmir: JAAC's planned 'Long March' to Muzaffarabad triggered deadly clashes killing 9-10, including a police officer, bringing the total dead since June to roughly 28-30.

16 Jul — Muzaffarabad: JAAC deferred the march to 21 July after backchannel talks reportedly involving Army Chief Asim Munir; Azad Kashmir elections remain set for 27 July with 16,000+ security personnel deployed.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, intensifying. Pakistan faces genuine three-front strain: Operation Shaban is likely to continue grinding through August with triple-digit militant death tolls, the 21 July march and 27 July AJK election are the two near-term flashpoints most likely to produce further fatalities, and the Afghan border front remains a latent risk that could reactivate with little warning.

countries.name: India
incidents: 1 Jul — Kamjong district, Manipur: Armed Naga-Kuki clashes near the Myanmar border torched 20+ houses in a tit-for-tat arson wave, tracing to the killing of six Naga civilians whose bodies were recovered 11 June.
Ongoing — Nagaland/Manipur border: NSCN(IM)'s 'Eastern Flank' continues to formally challenge the Hebron leadership over the 2015 Framework Agreement, a slower-burn risk to the Naga peace process.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Manipur's Naga-Kuki friction in Kamjong is likely to remain episodic rather than escalate to state-wide conflagration absent a mass-casualty trigger; the NSCN(IM) internal fracture bears watching as a potential complication to India's Naga peace process, though the LAC with China remains comparatively calm.

countries.name: China
incidents: 4 Jul — Taiwan's claimed EEZ: China Coast Guard rotated vessels into the zone, disabling Automatic Identification Systems, part of a continuous unmarked patrol posture running since 1 June.

6 Jul — South China Sea to Pacific: China conducted its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test (~7,300km), landing between Tuvalu and Kiribati, drawing formal pushback from the US, Japan, Australia and New Zealand.
6-13 Jul — Qingdao: 'Joint Sea-2026' naval exercise with Russia included 10 combined vessels, including submarines from both navies, followed by joint Pacific patrols.
7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Chinese and Japanese coast guard vessels faced off for roughly seven hours.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward the top of the band. Beijing is normalizing higher-order signaling — routine SLBM launches into international waters, continuous unmarked patrols near Taiwan, and combined exercises with Russia — as a steady-state 'new normal' rather than a discrete crisis; Senkaku standoffs are likely to keep recurring at similar frequency, with risk of miscalculation rising each time.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 8 Jul — Taiwan Strait/ADIZ: 22 PLA aircraft/UAV sorties were detected, 14 crossing the median line.
Mid-Jul — Taiwan Strait/ADIZ: A separate day saw 30 PLA sorties, 26 crossing the median line across four ADIZ sectors — among the month's highest single-day counts.
13 Jul — Nationwide: A one-week joint defense exercise served as prelude to Han Kuang Exercise No. 42, scheduled to begin 5 August.
Ongoing — Taiwan-wide: Prosecutors are investigating six individuals and searching Super Micro's Taiwan offices over alleged smuggling of ~$22 million in Nvidia GB300 chips into China.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. PLA pressure is likely to continue at an elevated-but-plateaued tempo rather than sharply escalating, as Beijing appears to be normalizing coercion ahead of Han Kuang 42 in August; watch for a spike in PLA activity deliberately timed against the exercise dates.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: H1 2026 — Scarborough Shoal: China Coast Guard patrol volume reached 933 ship-days, nearly matching all of 2025, with 112 days of direct CCG-PCG/BFAR interaction recorded.
23 Jun — Scarborough Shoal: The BRP Diego Silang was surrounded by four Chinese warships after China accused its helicopter of violating 'PRC airspace.'
7 Jul — Manila: Japan announced transfer of five Abukuma-class destroyer escorts to the Philippine Navy, deepening bilateral security cooperation.

Early-mid Jul — Multilateral: 14 nations plus the EU issued joint statements backing the 2016 South China Sea arbitral ruling, coinciding with the close of the three-month Salaknib 2026 exercise (7,000+ troops).
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Scarborough Shoal remains the primary flashpoint, with CCG patrol intensity likely to stay at or above 2025's already-elevated baseline; the Philippines continues leaning into multilateralization as its principal counter-pressure strategy, which somewhat lowers near-term escalation risk without resolving the underlying standoff.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 13 Jul — Nationwide: Cyberattacks on South Korea's military hit a five-year high — 18,951 attempted intrusions in 2025, up 31% year-on-year.
Jul — Nationwide: A North Korea-linked Kimsuky group attempted a spear-phishing attack on a defense-related organization using AI-generated deepfake imagery.
14 Jul — Seoul: North Korea publicly condemned growing Seoul-NATO cooperation and the US-ROK-Japan trilateral framework.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, driven primarily by North Korea's escalating cyber operations and fortification activity. Expect DPRK cyber operations against South Korean military and defense-industrial targets to keep intensifying, with AI-enabled deepfake and social-engineering tradecraft becoming standard rather than exceptional.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: Through 30 Apr — South China Sea: Chinese vessel transits within 10nm of Vietnamese-held features rose to 89 over the past 12 months, versus 50 three years prior.
Late Jun — Manila: President To Lam visited the Philippines, elevating the relationship to an 'enhanced' strategic partnership focused on South China Sea cooperation.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No new distinctly-dated Vietnam-China incident emerged this week, but the elevated intrusion trend continues; Vietnam is likely to continue quiet reinforcement of its own outposts alongside diplomatic hedging via the Philippines partnership rather than direct confrontation.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: A coast guard standoff with China lasted roughly seven hours, days after China's Pacific SLBM test.
7 Jul — Tokyo/Manila: Japan announced transfer of five Abukuma-class destroyer escorts to the Philippine Navy.

9 Jul — Tokyo: A governing-coalition partner (Japan Innovation Party) pushed recommendations for early introduction of nuclear-powered submarines and review of Japan's non-nuclear principles, explicitly framed around China's expansion.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, watch for upgrade. Senkaku confrontations are becoming a near-monthly pattern, and Japan's own posture is shifting toward more assertive self-reliant defense planning; the submarine/non-nuclear-principles debate is unlikely to produce a policy change within 90 days but will keep generating domestic political friction.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 2 Jul — Ipdeheik Airstrip, Yahukimo Regency: TPNPB fighters shot and killed American aid pilot Nicholas Gosselin and burned his aircraft, calling it a 'message' over pilots ferrying Indonesian troops.
3 Jul — Yahukimo: TNI conducted a special operation to recover the pilot's body.

8 Jul — Yahukimo: The manhunt for seven suspects continued, with an active TPNPB base discovered near the attack site; Papua security-incident counts have risen from 3 in early 2025 to 23 in the same period of 2026.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, validated by this week's events. Indonesia is likely to intensify TNI operations in Yahukimo as the manhunt continues, raising the risk of further clashes and displacement; the killing of a US national keeps this a live diplomatic issue, with aviation remaining a persistent vulnerability given the region's dependence on small aircraft.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Ongoing — Thailand-Cambodia border: No new dated incident was found this week; the ceasefire brokered after the July 2025 border war (38+ killed, 300,000 displaced) continues to hold, though sporadic incidents and mistrust remain.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Absent a new triggering incident, the border situation remains a low-simmer risk rather than active conflict; landmine incidents remain the most probable trigger for renewed escalation given the 2025 precedent.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: 6 Jul — Savar: Attackers hurled petrol bombs at a National Citizen Party opposition rally, wounding four activists.

Ongoing (June, feeding into July) — Nationwide: Rights group ASK recorded 66 political killings, 61 deaths in police custody and 11 extrajudicial killings over six months; 9 killed and 346 injured in 58 incidents in June alone.
Ongoing — Dhaka: 26 Awami League activists were arrested for defying the party's ban during its founding-anniversary observance; a Jamaat-e-Islami-led alliance staged a rival rally the same period.
countries.coa: Upgraded to MODERATE from LOW. Political violence is persisting and potentially intensifying under PM Tarique Rahman's BNP government despite a promise to end it; expect continued targeted attacks on rival-party officials and rallies, and periodic security clampdowns around banned Awami League activity.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 5-6 Jul — Negombo Prison: Rival inmate/gang clashes over drug-trafficking tensions killed at least 26-28, including 6-7 prison officers, and wounded 100+ — the deadliest prison violence in years, driven by severe overcrowding.
8 Jul — Boossa/Agunukolapelessa prisons: Two additional inmates transferred from Negombo died the same day; rights groups alleged torture of transferred inmates.
Mid-Jul — Colombo/Geneva: Amnesty International and the UN Human Rights Office demanded an urgent, independent investigation into both the riot and the subsequent transfer deaths.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, but institutionally significant. The Negombo fallout is shifting from a security incident into a human-rights and governance controversy; absent structural reform, Sri Lanka's chronic system-wide overcrowding (39,000 inmates vs. 10,000 capacity) makes a recurrence at another facility plausible.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Twin constitutional crises are testing hemispheric norms — Venezuela's expired interim mandate and Colombia's disputed presidential transition are both unresolved, with late July/early August (Venezuela's 28 Jul flashpoint, Colombia's 7 Aug handover, Peru's 28 Jul inauguration) shaping up as a concentrated window of hemispheric political risk.

Non-state armed actors are exploiting state capacity gaps — the ELN in Colombia, gangs in Haiti, and rival cartels in Ecuador are all expanding operational tempo precisely where state attention is diverted by disaster response, transition paralysis or under-resourced security forces.

Infrastructure and economic collapse are driving bottom-up unrest — Cuba's third total grid failure in nine days and Bolivia's currency float after weeks of blockades are both generating spontaneous street protest distinct from organized political opposition.

Extortion economies are maturing into open violence — Peru's transport-sector and school extortion wave and Ecuador's Manabí sicariato violence both show criminal economies shifting from covert intimidation to public, high-visibility attacks.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 11 Jul — Nationwide: The government raised the 24 June twin-earthquake death toll to 4,829, with tens of thousands still reported missing on a citizen-run tracking platform.

14 Jul — Caracas: National Assembly president Jorge Rodríguez announced a 'joint agenda' with opposition figure Dinorah Figuera framed around reconstruction, sidestepping the constitutional election mandate that lapsed 5 July.
15-16 Jul — La Guaira: Maduro's son was heckled by a grieving mother while touring a destroyed housing project, reflecting rising public anger — 63.3% disapprove of Rodríguez's earthquake response.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, pressure compounding. Rodríguez is likely to keep deferring a concrete election timeline citing reconstruction, facing mounting opposition and US-backed pressure via the Caracas talks track. The 28 July date (Chávez's birthday and the 2024 election anniversary) is a likely flashpoint for renewed street protest; watch for Machado's promised return from exile as a second destabilizing trigger.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 4-8 Jul — Kenscoff (Téléco, Robin): Gang attacks killed at least 8 residents and displaced roughly 5,840 people, per IOM.

14 Jul — New York: The UN Security Council heard that 1,642 have been killed and 745 injured in Haiti so far in 2026, plus a 120% rise in drone-attack fatalities (~1,200 killed by drones this year, including 17 children).
15 Jul — Santo Domingo: The Dominican Republic confirmed it will not send troops to the Gang Suppression Force, limiting its role to humanitarian, logistics and intelligence support.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Gang expansion into peripheral zones like Kenscoff is likely to keep producing mass-displacement events; the Gang Suppression Force remains understrength at roughly 1,000 of a planned 5,500, delaying any decisive counter-gang campaign, while drone-attack casualties continue rising as a distinct and growing lethality driver.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 7 Jul — Bogotá: President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella suspended the transition process, accusing outgoing President Petro of attempting a coup after Petro alleged unsubstantiated election fraud; observers found no evidence of fraud and the 7 August handover is still expected.
14 Jul — Chocó: The ELN abducted 39 civilians, including a minor, from a highway bus; the military launched a same-day rescue freeing all hostages, but 2 soldiers were killed in the operation.
14 Jul — Betoyes, Tame (Arauca): The ELN's Eastern War Front detonated a truck bomb at a police substation, killing 2 police officers and 1 civilian and wounding 9-10.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Expect continued political friction through the scheduled 7 August handover, with residual risk that Petro's fraud allegations reignite legitimacy disputes even absent evidence; the ELN is likely to sustain a high operational tempo in Arauca/Chocó border zones as the incoming government's security posture is tested early.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 14 Jul — Nationwide: Cuba's grid suffered its third total collapse in under 10 days, leaving roughly 10 million people without power, with some outages exceeding 24-87 hours across provinces.

15 Jul — Havana/Santiago de Cuba: Spontaneous 'cacerolazo' protests erupted, chanting 'turn on the lights' and 'Libertad,' met by intensified police presence and internet restrictions — the largest wave since July 2021.
Ongoing — Nationwide: Rights group Cubalex documented at least 38 arrests tied to the protests, including 6 minors, against a backdrop of nearly 2,000 repressive actions recorded in H1 2026.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, escalating within the band. Further total grid collapses are near-certain absent fuel relief, given a razor-thin generation margin (935MW capacity against ~3,100MW demand) and no sign the US oil blockade will ease; a single outage exceeding roughly 72 hours nationally would be the key threshold to watch for broader unrest.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 8 Jul — Manta (Santa Ana): Gunmen fired 60+ rounds at a wake, killing 4 — the 16th such attack in Manabí in 2026.
13-14 Jul — Sucre canton: A father and son were abducted from their home overnight and found the next morning tortured and shot dead.
13-16 Jul — Manta/Portoviejo: Separate targeted killings continued, including a man shot at a used-vehicle lot and a driver killed on the Portoviejo-El Rodeo road.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Manabí is likely to remain Ecuador's deadliest province as Los Lobos and Los Choneros continue contesting trafficking corridors; near-daily sicariato-style killings in Manta/Portoviejo are expected to persist despite Noboa's 60-day state of emergency across 10 provinces.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 12 Jul — Panamericana Norte: Extortionists opened fire on a touring cumbia band's bus, wounding 3.
14 Jul — Huaycán, Lima: Police caught an extortion cell in the act after they detonated a bomb at a gas-supply shop entrance.
16 Jul — Callao: A public-transport driver was shot up to 8 times by extortionists at the start of his shift.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained but watch-listed upward. Extortion-driven violence against transport workers and small businesses is likely to continue at a high tempo through Keiko Fujimori's 28 July inauguration, which is expected to bring renewed pressure for a security crackdown; structural reform is unlikely within 90 days.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 4 Jul — El Alto: Police arrested Vicente Salazar, top leader of the La Paz Túpac Katari farmers' federation, on terrorism/criminal-association charges tied to the blockade campaign.
6-9 Jul — La Paz: Prosecutors requested 6 months' pretrial detention for Salazar as formal hearing proceedings continued.
15 Jul — La Paz: IMF negotiations for a $3.3 billion loan program were expected to begin, though talks have reportedly stalled over currency-devaluation terms.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. With Salazar's prosecution proceeding and a state of emergency still in force, large-scale blockades are unlikely to resume immediately, but underlying grievances over the currency float and austerity terms remain unresolved; a collapse in IMF talks could re-trigger union mobilization within the window.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: 15 Jul — Santo Domingo: President Abinader confirmed the DR will not deploy troops to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force, limiting involvement to humanitarian and logistics support.

Ongoing — Nationwide: The government continues an aggressive deportation campaign against Haitian migrants (roughly 10,000/week), including raids on informal settlements, drawing sustained rights-group criticism.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No organized armed-conflict or mass-unrest indicators; the deportation campaign is likely to continue drawing international criticism without altering the DR's own low domestic-crime classification, with the Haiti border remaining the primary regional-stability risk vector.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 5-11 Jul — Nationwide: 16 murders were recorded for the week; the year-to-date total is down roughly 22-24% versus 2025 (291 vs. 374 at a comparable point).
Ongoing — Nationwide: The JCF reports a murder clearance rate of 111% year-to-date, a marked improvement over 71% the prior year.
countries.coa: LOW maintained and trending favorably. The homicide decline is likely to continue absent a major disruption, aided by improved clearance rates; the chief external risk is Jamaica's own troop commitment to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force.
